utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright brittany so i see that you are experiencing some right foot pain could you tell me what happened

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah well i was playing tennis and i was trying to you know volley the ball

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it was like a double game and i was trying to volley the ball and i got in front of another player and actually ended up falling on top of my foot

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and then yeah it kinda hurt i quickly then twisted my myself around her because i was trying to catch myself but then i started to feel some pain in my foot

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm okay have you ever injured that foot before

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah no sorry i injured my other foot before not this foot

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so right now you're experiencing right leg pain but you have injured your your left leg before is that what i'm hearing

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah that's fine

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright were you able to continue playing

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i had to stop i actually it was like i had to be held from the field because i could n't put weight on my foot

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i'm sorry okay so what have you been doing for the pain since then

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i wrapped it after a the game they had some ace wraps in their clubhouse and so i wrapped it up and then i iced it last night and i just kept it up on a pillow and then i took some ibuprofen

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay could you one more time when did this injury happen

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: this happened about couple days ago

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so did you say whether does the ibuprofen help at all

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah it helps a little bit but then you know it it you know after a while it wears out

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then have you experienced any numb numbness or tingling

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no no numbness

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright any loss in sensation

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i mean i i can still feel like i can still feel my foot

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright that's good to hear so you were playing tennis is that what you normally do to work out

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do i'm trying to learn but i can not afford tennis less lessons so me and my friends just hit the balls back and forth i do sleep

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i love it absolutely yeah my dad one time took me to play racquet ball and i learned the very bruisy way that that was n't for me yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that scares me

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it's it they they move pretty fast i'm not gon na lie alright so if you do n't mind i'm gon na go ahead and do my my physical exam i'm gon na be calling out some of my findings but if you have any questions go ahead stop me let me know but i will be explaining along the way okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright so i've looked at your vitals and honestly they look great you know your blood pressure i see is one twenty five over seventy that's almost textbook respiratory rate we are seeing you at a smooth eighteen excuse me your temperature you're running normal ninety seven . one you're you're satting at a hundred percent so and then your pulse so that's interesting like you're you're going at like about sixty beats a minute so i think they're i think we're doing pretty well i'm gon na go ahead and listen to your heart on your heart exam i do n't appreciate any like murmur rub or gallop we have a nice regular rate and rhythm for your lung exam i do appreciate a little bit of stridor that's really interesting but i do n't hear any wheezes or rales so that's great for your i know this sounds weird but for your abdominal exam i do n't appreciate any rebound no guarding on your skin exam i do n't sorry like on your your head everything looks symmetrical your your mucosal membranes are normal you do n't feel hot to touch so that's great but i'm gon na do my foot exam okay so on the right foot there is some bruising of the plantar and dorsal aspects of the foot there is associated swelling when i touch on your midfoot here does it hurt

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no uh uh

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright tenderness to palpation of the midfoot and positive piano key test of the first and second metatarsals alright it's also warm to touch alright so on your neurovascular exam of your right foot your capillary refill is less than three seconds strong dorsalis pedis pulse and your sensation is intact to light touch your left foot exam is normal capillary refill is appropriate pedal pulses are strong and sensation is intact so i know that before here we before i came in that we got an x-ray so i've reviewed the results of your x-ray of your right foot and it showed subtle dorsal displacement of the base of the second metatarsal with a three millimeter separation of the first and second metatarsal bases and the presence of a bony fragment in the lisfranc joint space alright i know those were a bunch of fancy words so now i'm gon na explain to you what that all means for my impression and plan your first problem is right foot pain consistent with a lisfranc fracture which is a fracture to one of your second metatarsal bones near the top of your foot right so the big part of your toe is the first metatarsal the second part where you can kinda like bend it right that's the that's the metatarsal that we're talking about based on your exam and what i'm seeing on your x-ray i am gon na recommend surgery for your foot the surgery will help place the bones in their proper positions using plates and screws to help prevent further complications there are also many ligaments at the top of your foot so i will be ordering an mri to further assess the fracture and any injury to the ligaments i know this is a lot do you have any questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah do i have to do the surgery

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so i'm recommending it as there can be significant complications to your foot if you do n't it can lead to poor bone alignment or poor ligament healing which can lead to you losing the arch of your foot and becoming flat-footed you can also develop arthritis in that foot so yes i i i highly recommend it if you want to be able to walk and move about in a way that you are familiar with

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i just hate that word surgery doc

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i know

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know it scares me every time i mean especially with my foot i want to be able to walk again and so i just get really worried i mean how long is the procedure usually too

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so it's actually

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: have to be in the hospital

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: no no no no no it's actually a day surgery and you'll be able to go home the same day and then you will follow up with me here in the clinic in about a week you'll be in a cast and you will use crutches as you will not be able to use that foot for six to eight weeks after that you'll start gradually walking on your foot based on how you do so the procedure itself is not very long you will and so like since you will be able to go home that's great but you wo n't be able to drive especially since you're saying are you left handed or right handed

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm right handed

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah so your your right foot is probably your dominant one and the also the one you're supposed to drive with so no you're gon na you're gon na need somebody to take you home but what

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: uh uh

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: does that mean i'm out for the rest of the season i mean i wan na be able to get back and play again i really am i'm getting a little better so i

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i really wan na keep on playing my tennis with my friends but

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah so unfortunately yes it does mean that you're out for the rest of the season but hopefully we can get you a great get you to a set up well for next season and in the meantime i think i'm gon na recommend after surgery that we get you to physical therapy i think that that's gon na be a really great way to like kinda strengthen the muscles and make sure that you're at peak performance before we put you back out there

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i suppose so

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: no problem so i do wan na let you know that there are some risks associated with any kind of surgical procedure i'm gon na bring you some paperwork and that my ma is gon na go over with you such as like risks of bleeding loss of sensation nerve damage all those things will be discussed with you and if you have any questions leading up to and even after your procedure go ahead and ask them and we'll be more than happy to help with that okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: good

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: thank you

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: thank you

detail_list.summary: Right foot pain.
The patient was playing tennis when the injury occurred.
The patient fell on the dorsal aspect of her right foot while trying to volley a tennis ball.
The patient twisted her foot while trying to catch herself.
The doctor asks if the patient has injured the foot before.
The patient has a history of a left leg injury.
The doctor asks if the patient was able to continue playing tennis.
The patient was unable to continue playing due to inability to bear weight on the foot.
The doctor asks what the patient has done for the pain.
The patient wrapped the foot, iced it, elevated it on a pillow, and took ibuprofen.
The doctor asks for the duration of the injury.
The injury occurred two days ago.
The doctor asks if the ibuprofen helps with the pain.
The patient reports ibuprofen provides temporary relief.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced numbness or tingling.
The patient denies numbness.
The doctor asks if there is any loss of sensation.
The patient denies loss of sensation.
The doctor declares the intent to perform a physical examination.
The doctor reviews normal vital signs and performs a physical exam of the right foot, noting bruising, swelling, midfoot tenderness, positive piano key test, warmth, and intact neurovascular status.
The doctor reviews x-ray findings of a Lisfranc fracture and recommends surgery.
The patient asks if surgery is necessary.
The doctor explains the risks of not having surgery, including poor bone alignment, loss of foot arch, and arthritis.
The patient asks about the duration of the surgical procedure.
The surgery is an outpatient procedure, followed by a one-week follow-up, six to eight weeks of non-weight bearing in a cast, and subsequent physical therapy.
The patient is right-handed.
The patient asks if she will be unable to play tennis for the rest of the season.
The doctor confirms the patient will be out for the season and recommends physical therapy post-surgery.
The doctor discusses surgical risks including bleeding, loss of sensation, and nerve damage.
